questions.text: Displacement is
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: A dimension is a measurable extent of some kind, such as length, breadth, depth, or height.
A scalar quantity has only magnitude and no direction. Displacement requires both magnitude and direction.
Displacement is a vector quantity because it involves both magnitude and direction.
Speed is a scalar quantity that refers to how fast an object is moving, not its overall change in position.

questions.explanation: Displacement is defined as a vector quantity because it considers both the magnitude and direction of an object's change in position from its initial point to its final point. This is distinct from scalar quantities like distance, which only consider magnitude. Option A, 'Dimension,' refers to a physical measurement and not a type of quantity applicable to displacement. Option B, 'Scalar,' is incorrect because displacement requires direction, which scalars do not have. Option D, 'Speed,' is a scalar and only measures how fast something is moving, without involving direction, making it unrelated to displacement.

questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: If the slope is constant, the velocity is constant. An increasing slope indicates a changing (increasing) velocity.
An increasing slope means the rate of change of displacement over time is increasing, indicating a greater average velocity.
An increasing slope indicates increasing velocity, which suggests acceleration is occurring, not decreasing.
A decreasing velocity would correspond to a decreasing slope, not an increasing one.

questions.explanation: In a displacement-time graph, the slope or gradient of the tangent line represents the velocity. An increasing slope means that the displacement is changing at an increasing rate over time, which indicates that velocity is increasing. Therefore, the average velocity becomes greater. Option B is correct. Options A and D are incorrect because they describe scenarios where the slope is constant or decreasing, respectively. Option C is incorrect because an increasing slope suggests that acceleration is positive, not decreasing.

questions.text: The force which produces acceleration of 1m/sec2 in a body of mass 1 Kg is equal to
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: A dyne is a smaller unit of force used in the centimeter-gram-second system. It is not used in the SI system where the problem is set.
One Newton is the correct SI unit of force required to accelerate a 1 kg mass by 1 m/s² according to Newton's Second Law of Motion.
The kilogram is a unit of mass, not force, and does not apply to this problem.
10 dyne is still a much smaller force than 1 Newton. The problem requires using the SI unit of force.

questions.explanation: According to Newton's Second Law of Motion, force is calculated as the product of mass and acceleration (F = ma). With a mass of 1 kg and an acceleration of 1 m/s², the force required is 1 Newton, which is the SI unit for force. Option A (One dyne) is incorrect because dyne is a smaller unit of force in the centimeter-gram-second system. Option C (10 Kg) is incorrect because kilogram is a unit of mass, not force. Option D (10 dyne) is incorrect as it represents a much smaller force than 1 Newton.

questions.text: The value of μk is
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Kinetic friction coefficient is typically not equal to static friction coefficient due to differing conditions.
The kinetic friction coefficient is usually less than the static friction coefficient because it takes more force to start moving an object than to keep it moving.
While kinetic friction can be less than static friction, it is not 'less and equal' simultaneously.

questions.explanation: The coefficient of kinetic friction (μk) is generally less than the coefficient of static friction (μs) because it usually requires more force to overcome static friction and start motion than to maintain motion against kinetic friction. Option A is incorrect because μk is not equal to μs; they represent different states of motion. Option C suggests an incorrect expression; it cannot be 'less and equal' at the same time. Option D is incorrect because it is easier to keep an object moving (kinetic friction) than to start it moving (static friction).

questions.text: When a space ship is out of the pull of the earth
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: In the absence of external forces, an object in space continues to move at a constant velocity due to Newton's first law of motion.
If the spaceship had zero velocity, it would be stationary, which is not the case if it's already in motion.
In space, without external forces like friction or gravity, the velocity of an object does not change.
Speed does not decrease unless there is an opposing force applied to the object.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is that the spaceship moves with a constant velocity. According to Newton's first law of motion, an object will remain in its state of rest or uniform motion unless acted upon by an external force. In space, once a spaceship is beyond the Earth's gravitational pull and no other forces are acting on it, it will continue to move at a constant velocity. The other options are incorrect because they suggest changes in velocity or speed, which would require the presence of unbalanced forces.

questions.text: The force of friction on a car wheel, on a road, of mass "m" is
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option fails to include the gravitational force, which is necessary to determine the normal force on a flat surface.
This option incorrectly multiplies the gravitational acceleration by itself, resulting in an overestimation of the frictional force.
This option correctly applies the formula for frictional force on a flat surface, considering both the coefficient of friction and the normal force, which is equal to the gravitational force mg.
This option incorrectly uses the square of mass, which is irrelevant in calculating frictional force.

questions.explanation: The correct answer isμmg. The frictional force (f) on a flat surface is calculated using the formula f = μN, where μ is the coefficient of friction and N is the normal force. On a flat surface, the normal force is equal to the gravitational force exerted by the mass of the car, N = mg. Therefore, the frictional force is f = μmg. The optionmμis incorrect because it ignores the gravitational component necessary for calculating the normal force. The optionμmg2overestimates the frictional force by including an extra factor of g. Finally,μm2is incorrect because squaring the mass is not relevant to calculating frictional force.

questions.subject: Physics

questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Speed refers to how fast something is moving, but it does not account for direction, which is essential for calculating velocity.

Displacement is the straight-line distance between the initial and final positions and includes direction, making it the correct factor for determining average velocity.
Velocity is a vector quantity that includes both speed and direction, but the question asks for the rate of change, which is specific to displacement when calculating average velocity.

Speed is a scalar quantity and does not involve direction, which is necessary for velocity calculations. Torque is related to rotational forces and not directly relevant to linear motion. questions.text: Motion along a line is called
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This refers to motion that occurs along a single straight line, requiring only one coordinate to describe its position.
This type of motion involves movement along a plane, requiring two coordinates to describe the position, such as movement on a flat surface like a table.
This motion takes place in space and requires three coordinates to describe the position, like the movement of an airplane in the sky.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is 'Motion in one dimension' because motion along a line only requires a single coordinate to describe its position. This is distinct from 'Motion in two dimensions,' which involves a plane and requires two coordinates, and 'Motion in three dimensions,' which involves space and requires three coordinates. 'None of these' is incorrect since the correct option is provided.

questions.text: The mass of an object is a quantitative measure of it's
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Force is an interaction that changes the motion of an object, not a measure of its mass.
Velocity describes the speed and direction of an object's motion, not its mass.
Inertia is the property of an object to resist changes in its state of motion, which is directly related to its mass.
Not all these terms are measures of mass. Inertia is the only one directly related to mass.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Inertia. Mass is a measure of an object's inertia, which is its tendency to resist changes to its motion. The greater the mass, the greater the inertia. This means more force is required to change the motion of an object with more mass. Force is not a measure of mass; it is an influence that can change the motion of an object. Velocity is the speed of something in a given direction and is unrelated to mass. The option All of them is incorrect because only inertia is a measure of mass.

questions.text: A stone has a mass of 40 grams, It weight is
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option assumes a much larger mass or gravitational acceleration, which is not applicable here.
This is the correct calculation using Earth's gravitational acceleration of approximately 9.8 m/s².
Dynes are a unit of force in the CGS system. Converting 0.392 N to dynes would yield a different value.
This option likely results from a misunderstanding or miscalculation of the weight formula.

questions.explanation: To find the weight of the stone, we use the formula Weight = mass × gravitational acceleration. The mass of the stone is 40 grams, which is 0.04 kg (since 1 kg = 1000 grams). The standard gravitational acceleration on Earth is approximately 9.8 m/s². Thus, the weight = 0.04 kg × 9.8 m/s² = 0.392 N. Option B is correct. Option A assumes an incorrect scenario, Option C uses a different unit (dynes), which isn't the correct conversion, and Option D reflects a miscalculation.

questions.text: An object whose mass is 100 gm starts from rest and moves with constant acceleration of 20 cm sec2 at the end 8 sec its momentum is in gm. cm /sec
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Correct! The momentum is calculated using the formula: momentum = mass × velocity.
This answer might result from a miscalculation. Ensure you correctly apply the formula for velocity.
This value is incorrect. Remember that velocity is not simply the acceleration value.
This is an incorrect calculation. Consider checking your final velocity computation.

questions.explanation: To find the momentum, we first calculate the velocity of the object at the end of 8 seconds using the formula: velocity = initial velocity + (acceleration × time). Since the object starts from rest, the initial velocity is 0. Therefore, velocity = 0 + (20 cm/s² × 8 s) = 160 cm/s. The momentum is then calculated using the formula: momentum = mass × velocity. Here, the mass is 100 g and the velocity is 160 cm/s, thus the momentum = 100 g × 160 cm/s = 16000 g·cm/s. Other options are incorrect due to errors in calculating velocity or improperly applying the momentum formula.

questions.text: The force which will accelerate a 10 Kg mass at 98 cm/sec2 on a smooth horizontal plane is
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Correct! Using the formula F = m * a, where m = 10 kg and a = 98 cm/sec2 (which is 0.98 m/sec2), the force is 9.8 N.
Incorrect. This would be the force if the acceleration was 9.8 m/sec2, not 0.98 m/sec2.
Incorrect. This result mistakenly uses an incorrect conversion or calculation of acceleration.
Incorrect. This is not derived from the correct calculation of the force using the given mass and acceleration.

questions.explanation: To find the force, we use the formula F = m * a, where F is the force, m is the mass, and a is the acceleration. Given that the mass (m) is 10 kg and the acceleration (a) is 98 cm/sec2, we first convert acceleration to meters per second squared: 98 cm/sec2 = 0.98 m/sec2. Therefore, F = 10 kg * 0.98 m/sec2 = 9.8 N. The correct answer is 9.8 N. Other options are incorrect due to either wrong conversion of units or incorrect multiplication.

questions.text: A string passes over a smooth pulley and carries a 2 Kg mass at one end and 1 Kg mass at the other. What is the acceleration of the masses?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This is the correct acceleration, calculated by considering the net force acting on the system and using Newton's second law.
This option is incorrect because it represents the acceleration due to gravity, not the net acceleration of the system.
This is incorrect and might be a miscalculation; it could be confused with a different scenario involving gravity.
This value is unrealistically high for this scenario and suggests a misunderstanding of the forces involved.

questions.explanation: To find the acceleration of the system, first identify the forces acting on the masses. For the 2 Kg mass, the force is 2g (where g = 9.8 m/s²), and for the 1 Kg mass, the force is 1g. The net force on the system is the difference in these gravitational forces, which is (2g - 1g) = 1g. Applying Newton's second law (F = ma) to the entire system (total mass = 2 Kg + 1 Kg = 3 Kg), we get:Net force = total mass × acceleration => 1g = 3aTherefore, a = g/3 = 9.8/3 ≈ 3.27 m/s².Option A is correct as it reflects this calculation. Option B represents the acceleration due to gravity and is not applicable here. Option C is incorrect as it does not match the calculated value, and Option D is an unrealistic and incorrect value for this situation.

questions.text: If the change in momentum with respect ot time is zero then
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: If a force acts, there would be a change in momentum over time, which contradicts the condition given in the question.
Momentum being zero is not necessarily related to the change in momentum over time being zero. Momentum can be zero or non-zero while still being constant.
When the change in momentum over time is zero, it implies that the momentum does not change, meaning it is constant. This is the correct interpretation.
If the force changes, it would result in a change in momentum over time, which is opposite to the condition provided in the question.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is that the momentum is constant. The rate of change of momentum with respect to time is equal to the force acting on the object according to Newton's second law. If this rate of change is zero, it means that no net force is acting on the object, thus the momentum remains constant. Option A is incorrect because if a force acts, it would cause a change in momentum. Option B incorrectly assumes that zero change in momentum implies zero momentum, which is not necessarily true. Option D is incorrect because a changing force would imply a changing momentum.

questions.text: When a falling object reaches a speed where the force of ait resistance is equal to its weight it
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Flying typically refers to moving through the air using wings or propulsion, not reaching a balance between forces.
Terminal velocity is reached when the force of air resistance equals the weight of the object, resulting in a constant speed.
The object will not slow down because the forces are balanced, causing it to move at a constant speed.
Zero velocity implies no movement, but the object is moving at a constant speed when forces are balanced.

questions.explanation: When an object falls through the air, gravity pulls it downward, increasing its speed. As its speed increases, the force of air resistance also increases. Eventually, the force of air resistance becomes equal to the object's weight. At this point, the object stops accelerating and continues to fall at a constant speed, known as terminal velocity. Therefore, option B is correct. Option A is incorrect because 'flying' involves a different concept of movement. Option C is wrong because the object does not slow down; it maintains a constant speed. Option D is incorrect because the object is not stationary; it is moving at a terminal velocity.

questions.text: the bullet fired from a gun has momentum, which is
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: According to the law of conservation of momentum, the total momentum before and after firing must be equal. Before firing, the system (gun plus bullet) is at rest, so their combined momentum is zero. After firing, the momentum of the bullet is equal and opposite to the momentum of the gun's recoil, maintaining the total momentum at zero.
This option is incorrect because if the bullet's momentum were less than the gun's recoil, it would violate the law of conservation of momentum.
This option is incorrect because if the bullet's momentum were greater than the gun's recoil, it would violate the law of conservation of momentum.
This option is incorrect because the scenario must obey the law of conservation of momentum, which dictates that the bullet's momentum and the gun's recoil are equal and opposite.

questions.explanation: When a bullet is fired from a gun, the gun and bullet exert equal and opposite forces on each other. According to the law of conservation of momentum, the momentum of the bullet is equal in magnitude and opposite in direction to the momentum of the gun's recoil. This ensures that the total momentum of the system (bullet and gun) remains zero, as it was before the firing. Therefore, the bullet's momentum is equal to the recoil of the gun. Other options suggesting different momenta would violate this fundamental principle of physics.

questions.text: If the values of instantaneous and average velocities are equal, the body is said to be moving with
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Uniform acceleration means the velocity is changing at a constant rate, so instantaneous velocity may not equal average velocity.
Uniform speed refers to constant speed, but it doesn't imply a constant direction, so the velocity could change.
Variable velocity means the velocity is changing over time, hence instantaneous and average velocities cannot be equal.
Uniform velocity means both the speed and direction are constant, resulting in instantaneous velocity being equal to average velocity.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Uniform velocity. When a body moves with uniform velocity, it means that both its speed and direction are constant over time. As a result, the instantaneous velocity at any point in time is equal to the average velocity over any time interval. Conversely, with Uniform acceleration, the velocity changes over time, so instantaneous and average velocities differ. Uniform speed only implies constant speed, not direction, so velocity can vary. Variable velocity explicitly means the velocity is changing, making the instantaneous and average velocities different.

questions.text: Acceleration of bodies of different masses allowed to fall freely is
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Acceleration due to gravity is constant for all heights near Earth's surface.
In the absence of air resistance, all bodies fall at the same acceleration due to gravity.
All objects, regardless of mass, experience the same acceleration when falling freely under gravity.
The acceleration due to gravity is not variable for freely falling objects near Earth's surface.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is that the acceleration of bodies of different masses allowed to fall freely is the same. This is due to the principle of uniform acceleration under gravity, which states that all objects experience the same gravitational acceleration, approximately 9.81 m/s² near the surface of the Earth, regardless of their mass. Option A is incorrect because the acceleration due to gravity is constant near the Earth's surface. Option B is incorrect because mass does not affect the acceleration of freely falling bodies in a vacuum. Option D is incorrect because the acceleration due to gravity is not variable but constant in such conditions.

questions.text: If a body is moving with constant acceleration, then
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Constant acceleration doesn't necessarily imply a change in direction; it depends on the initial direction and any forces acting on the body.
If the body has constant acceleration, it means the acceleration is not zero; the velocity is changing at a consistent rate.
With constant acceleration, the velocity changes at a steady rate. This means the velocity can increase if the acceleration is in the same direction as the velocity.
There is a correct statement among the options; hence 'None of these' is incorrect.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Option C: Its velocity is increasing. When a body moves with constant acceleration, its velocity changes uniformly over time. If the acceleration is positive and in the direction of motion, the velocity increases. Option A is incorrect because constant acceleration does not inherently imply a change in direction. Option B is incorrect because constant acceleration means the acceleration is not zero; rather, it is steady. Option D is incorrect because there is a correct choice among the provided options.

questions.text: If a body is moving along circle with constant angular velocity, then
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This would mean the body moves at a constant speed along the circle, which is not the case since direction changes.
Correct. Even though the speed remains constant, the direction of the velocity vector changes continuously, so the linear velocity is not uniform.
This is incorrect. The body has a centripetal acceleration due to the constant change in direction of motion.
This is incorrect. The body moves with a constant angular velocity, which means angular acceleration is zero.

questions.explanation: In circular motion with constant angular velocity, the magnitude of the linear velocity (speed) is constant, but its direction changes at every point along the circle. This means the linear velocity vector is not uniform. The body experiences centripetal acceleration, directed towards the center of the circle, which keeps changing the direction of the velocity vector. Therefore, the statement that 'its linear velocity is changing' is correct. Option A is incorrect because while the speed is constant, the velocity is not due to the changing direction. Option C is incorrect as there is centripetal acceleration. Option D is incorrect because with constant angular velocity, angular acceleration is zero.

questions.text: If a body is moving with a constant speed in a circle, then its centripetal acceleration is directed
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This is correct because centripetal acceleration always points towards the center of the circle, maintaining the circular motion.
This is incorrect. If the acceleration were away from the center, the object would move out of its circular path.
This is incorrect. Tangential direction is associated with velocity, not acceleration, in uniform circular motion.
Since there is a correct answer among the options, this choice is incorrect.

questions.explanation: In uniform circular motion, an object moves with a constant speed along a circular path. Despite the constant speed, the direction of the object's velocity is continuously changing, which means there is an acceleration. This acceleration, known as centripetal acceleration, is directed towards the center of the circle. This inward acceleration is necessary to keep the object moving in its circular path. Option A is correct because it describes this inward direction. Option B is incorrect as acceleration away from the center would not sustain circular motion. Option C is incorrect because tangential acceleration would change the speed, not the direction, and the object is moving at constant speed. Option D is incorrect because an accurate answer is provided in the options.

questions.text: Laws of motion are valid in a frame of reference which is
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: An inertial frame of reference is one that is either at rest or moves at a constant velocity, where Newton's laws of motion are valid without corrections.
Non-inertial frames are accelerating frames where fictitious forces, such as the Coriolis force, need to be considered to apply Newton's laws.

An accelerated frame of reference is a type of non-inertial frame where additional forces are required to account for the acceleration, making it unsuitable for the straightforward application of Newton's laws.
This option is incorrect because there is a valid frame, the inertial frame, where Newton's laws of motion can be applied directly.

questions.explanation: Newton's laws of motion are applicable in an inertial frame of reference. An inertial frame is one that is either at rest or moving with a constant velocity, meaning it is not accelerating. In such frames, the laws of motion hold without the need for additional forces. Non-inertial frames, on the other hand, are accelerating and require the introduction of fictitious forces to apply Newton's laws correctly. Therefore, the correct answer is 'Inertial'.

questions.text: Laws of motion are not valid in a system which is
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: An object at rest can still be within an inertial frame, where Newton's laws apply.
An inertial frame of reference is one where Newton's laws of motion are valid.
A non-inertial frame of reference is accelerating, where Newton's laws do not hold without additional pseudo-forces.
An object moving with uniform velocity is in an inertial frame, where Newton's laws are applicable.

questions.explanation: Newton's laws of motion are applicable in inertial frames of reference, which are either at rest or moving with a constant velocity. In a non-inertial frame, which is accelerating, these laws do not hold unless additional forces, known as pseudo-forces, are introduced to account for the acceleration. Therefore, option C is correct. Options A and D describe conditions that are consistent with an inertial frame, while option B directly states an inertial frame, all of which validate Newton's laws.

questions.text: The laws of motion give the relation between
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. While mass and velocity are components of momentum, they are not directly related by the laws of motion.
This is the correct option. Newton's second law of motion states that force is equal to the mass of an object multiplied by its acceleration (F = ma).
This option is incorrect. Velocity and acceleration are related through kinematic equations, but the laws of motion specifically relate force and acceleration.
This option is incorrect. Mass and weight are related by the force of gravity, where weight is the force exerted by gravity on mass, but this relationship is not described by the laws of motion.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Option B: Force and acceleration. Newton's second law of motion provides a direct relationship between the force applied to an object and the acceleration it experiences, expressed as F = ma, where F is force, m is mass, and a is acceleration. Option A is incorrect as it refers to momentum, not the laws of motion. Option C is incorrect because velocity and acceleration are related through kinematics, not directly by the laws of motion. Option D is incorrect because it pertains to the gravitational force on an object, not the laws of motion.

questions.text: Newton's second law of motion is also called
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Newton's second law states that the acceleration of an object is directly proportional to the net force acting on it and inversely proportional to its mass. This is commonly referred to as the Law of Acceleration.
This is actually Newton's first law, which states that an object will remain at rest or in uniform motion unless acted upon by a net force.
This refers to Newton's law of universal gravitation, which explains the gravitational attraction between masses.
The laws of thermodynamics deal with the principles of heat, energy, and work, not motion and force.

questions.explanation: Newton's second law of motion, known as the Law of Acceleration, describes the relationship between the force applied to an object, its mass, and the resulting acceleration. It can be mathematically expressed as F = ma, where F is the force applied, m is the mass, and a is the acceleration. The other options are related to different scientific principles: the Law of Inertia refers to Newton's first law, the Law of Gravitation pertains to the gravitational attraction between masses, and the Laws of Thermodynamics are concerned with heat and energy, not motion.

questions.text: The force acting on a body of mass 10 Kg falling under gravity is

This value represents the acceleration due to gravity, not the force.
A non-zero force acts on the object due to gravity.

questions.text: A body of mass 5 Kg starts from rest and falls freely. The distance covered by it is one second is

The distance s covered by the body in time t is given by the equation s = 0.5 * g * t². Substituting the given values, g = 9.8 m/s² and t = 1 second, we find s = 0.5 * 9.8 * (1)² = 4.9 meters. Therefore, the correct answer is 4.9 m.The other options are incorrect because they either use the wrong formula or incorrectly apply the concept of acceleration due to gravity.

questions.text: A body is moving in a circle at a constant speed. Which of the following statement is true?

options.text: There is no force acting on it
options.explanation: A force is necessary to change the direction of the body and maintain circular motion.

options.text: There is force acting at a tangent to the circle
options.explanation: While there may be a component of velocity along the tangent, the force maintaining the circular motion is directed inward.

options.text: There is force acting towards the center of the circle
options.explanation: This is the centripetal force required to keep the body in circular motion.

questions.explanation: In circular motion, even if the speed is constant, the direction of the velocity is continuously changing. This change in direction constitutes an acceleration, known as centripetal acceleration, which always points towards the center of the circle. Consequently, there must be a net force acting inward, called the centripetal force, to maintain this motion. Option A is incorrect because the body does experience acceleration due to the continuous change in direction. Option B is incorrect because a force is needed to sustain the circular motion. Option C is incorrect because the force acting on the body is directed towards the center, not tangent to the circle.

questions.text: When a force is applied to a body, several effects are possible. Which of the following effect could not occur?

A force can cause a body to accelerate, resulting in an increase in speed.

options.text: The mass of the body decreases
options.explanation: The mass of a body is not affected by external forces; it remains constant unless matter is added or removed.
A force can alter the direction of a body's motion without changing its speed.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Option C: The mass of the body decreases. The mass of an object is an intrinsic property that does not change when a force is applied. Forces can change an object's state of motion, causing it to rotate, accelerate, or change direction, but they do not alter the mass. Option A, B, and D describe possible effects of force: rotation, acceleration (speeding up), and change of direction, respectively. None of these effects involve a change in mass.

questions.text: The braking force needed to bring a car of mass 1200 kg to rest in 5 seconds when it is moving at 20 m/s is
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This value assumes a different deceleration than what is calculated using the given parameters.
Correct! This is the result of calculating the force needed to stop the car using the car's mass, initial velocity, and stopping time.
This value is half of the correct force, which indicates a miscalculation in either deceleration or mass.
This value is too low and indicates a misunderstanding of the relationship between force, mass, and acceleration.

questions.explanation: To find the braking force, you can use Newton's second law, F = ma, where F is the force, m is the mass, and a is the acceleration. First, calculate the acceleration using the formula: a = (final velocity - initial velocity) / time. Since the car comes to rest, the final velocity is 0 m/s. Thus, a = (0 m/s - 20 m/s) / 5 s = -4 m/s². The negative sign indicates deceleration. Now, apply F = ma: F = 1200 kg * (-4 m/s²) = -4800 N. The negative sign indicates that the force is in the opposite direction of the motion, which is braking. Therefore, the magnitude of the braking force needed is 4800 N. Other options are incorrect as they result from either incorrect calculations or misunderstandings of the concepts of deceleration and force.

questions.text: A body whose momentum is constant must have constant
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: If the momentum is constant and the mass is unchanging, the velocity must also remain constant.
Force is the rate of change of momentum. If the momentum is constant, the net force must be zero, not constant.
Acceleration is the rate of change of velocity. Constant momentum implies zero acceleration, not constant acceleration.
This option suggests that no quantity is constant, which is incorrect as velocity must be constant for momentum to remain unchanged.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Option A: Velocity. Momentum is defined as the product of mass and velocity (p = mv). If the momentum of a body is constant and assuming the mass does not change, then the velocity must remain constant as well. Option B: Force is incorrect because force is related to the change in momentum. A constant momentum implies that no external net force is acting on the body (net force is zero).Option C: Acceleration is incorrect because a change in velocity would indicate a change in momentum. Constant momentum means zero acceleration.Option D: None of these is incorrect because, as explained, the velocity must be constant.

questions.text: When a horse pulls a cart, the force that causes the horse to move forward is the force
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: The cart exerts on the horse
options.explanation: This is the force of friction from the cart, which resists the horse's forward motion rather than aiding it.

options.text: The horse exerts on the cart
options.explanation: This force is what the horse uses to pull the cart forward, but does not explain how the horse itself moves forward.

options.text: The horse exerts on the ground
options.explanation: This describes the action force: the horse pushes the ground backwards, setting up the scenario for the reaction force.

options.text: The ground exerts on the horse
options.explanation: According to Newton's third law of motion, this is the reaction force that propels the horse forward, enabling movement.

questions.explanation: According to Newton's third law of motion, every action has an equal and opposite reaction. When the horse pushes backward on the ground, this is the action force. In response, the ground exerts an equal and opposite reaction force on the horse, which is what propels the horse forward. Option A describes the frictional force opposing the horse's motion, Option B describes the force exerted on the cart, which does not move the horse, and Option C describes the action force without the corresponding reaction. Therefore, Option D correctly identifies the force that moves the horse forward.

questions.text: Motion of rocket in space (vacuum) is the example of
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Newton's First Law states that an object will remain at rest or in uniform motion unless acted upon by an external force. It describes the persistence of the rocket's motion in space.

Newton's Second Law states that the acceleration of an object is dependent on the net force acting on it and inversely proportional to its mass. It explains how the rocket accelerates in space when thrust is applied.
Newton's Third Law states that for every action, there is an equal and opposite reaction. This is crucial for the rocket's propulsion mechanism in space.

All three of Newton's laws are needed to fully describe the motion of a rocket in space. The first law explains its constant velocity, the second law explains its change in velocity, and the third law explains its propulsion.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is that the motion of a rocket in space involves all three of Newton's laws of motion. The First Law explains that the rocket will continue moving in a straight line at constant speed unless acted upon by another force. The Second Law describes how the rocket's motion changes when a force, such as thrust, is applied. The Third Law is directly involved in the rocket's propulsion, as the expulsion of gas out of the engine creates an equal and opposite reaction that propels the rocket forward. Therefore, understanding the motion of a rocket in space requires recognizing the application of all three laws.

questions.text: The rate of change of linear momentum is known as
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Weight is the force exerted by gravity on an object, not related to the rate of change of momentum.
Force is the correct answer because it is defined as the rate of change of momentum according to Newton's second law.
Mass is a measure of the amount of matter in an object, and it remains constant regardless of the object's motion.
Acceleration is the rate of change of velocity, not momentum. However, it is related to force through Newton's second law.

questions.explanation: The rate of change of linear momentum is known as force, as stated by Newton's second law of motion. This law is typically expressed as F = dp/dt, where F is force, p is momentum, and t is time. Weight is the gravitational force acting on an object, and while it is a type of force, it specifically relates to gravity, not momentum change. Mass is a scalar quantity representing the amount of matter in an object and does not change with motion. Acceleration is the rate of change of velocity over time and contributes to the change in momentum but is not the definition of force.

questions.text: A ball is hit so that it moves straight up in air and reaches the maximum height in 2 seconds its initial velocity is
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This is the correct initial velocity required for the ball to reach its maximum height in 2 seconds under Earth's gravity. We use the equation: final velocity = initial velocity - (gravity × time).
This velocity would be correct if the ball took 4 seconds to reach the maximum height. For 2 seconds, it's too high.
This velocity would result in the ball reaching its peak height in 1 second, not 2 seconds. It's too low for the given time.
This velocity is too low and would result in the ball reaching its peak height in less than 1 second.

questions.explanation: To determine the initial velocity of the ball, we use the kinematic equation where the final velocity at the peak of its trajectory is zero: final velocity = initial velocity - (acceleration due to gravity × time). At maximum height, the final velocity is 0 m/s. Therefore, the equation is 0 = initial velocity - (9.8 m/s² × 2 s). Solving for initial velocity gives us 19.6 m/s, which is Option A. The other options are incorrect because they do not satisfy the required equation for the given time of 2 seconds. Option B assumes a longer time, Option C assumes a shorter time, and Option D is incorrect for both time and gravity conditions.

questions.text: Earth is taken to be a
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: A non-inertial frame is one that is accelerating or rotating. While Earth does rotate and revolve, for many physics problems, it is considered approximately inertial.

An inertial frame is one where Newton's laws of motion hold true without requiring correction for acceleration or rotation. Although Earth does move, for most practical purposes in physics, it is considered an inertial frame.
Reference axes are a set of axes used to measure position or motion, not a type of frame. Earth itself is not a set of axes.
This option implies none of the provided options are correct, which is not the case.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is that Earth is taken to be an inertial frame. This assumption simplifies many physics problems by allowing us to apply Newton's laws of motion without accounting for the complexities of Earth's rotation and revolution. While technically Earth is not a perfect inertial frame due to its rotation and orbit, these effects are often negligible for many practical physics problems. Option A is incorrect because a non-inertial frame involves acceleration, which complicates the application of Newton's laws. Option C is incorrect because reference axes are not a type of frame. Option D is incorrect because one of the defined options is indeed correct.

questions.text: In the absence of any force acting on a body
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: Its momentum changes due to a force acting on the body
options.explanation: This option is incorrect because it contradicts the given condition of no external force acting on the body. Momentum changes only when an external force is applied.
This is the correct answer. According to the conservation of momentum, a body's momentum remains constant in the absence of external forces, aligning with Newton's First Law.
This option is incorrect because it suggests that momentum can change without external forces, which violates the principle of momentum conservation.
This option is incorrect as it implies that momentum changes over time without any external influence, which is contrary to the laws of physics described by Newton.

questions.explanation: In the absence of any external force acting on a body, its momentum remains constant. This is due to Newton's First Law of Motion, which states that an object will remain at rest or in uniform motion unless acted upon by an external force. Consequently, without any external force, there is no change in momentum. Option B is correct as it accurately reflects the principle of conservation of momentum. Options A, C, and D are incorrect because they imply changes in momentum that would require external forces, which are not present in this scenario.

questions.text: The SI unit of linear momentum is
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: N m (Newton-meter) is the unit of torque, not momentum.
N-m2 (Newton-square meter) is not a standard unit in physics and is not related to momentum.
N/m (Newton per meter) is the unit of stiffness or spring constant, not momentum.
N-s (Newton-second) is the correct unit of linear momentum, derived from the formula momentum = mass × velocity.

questions.explanation: Linear momentum is defined as the product of an object's mass and its velocity. The unit of mass is kilograms (kg) and the unit of velocity is meters per second (m/s), resulting in the unit kg m/s for momentum. This is equivalent to a Newton-second (N-s), since 1 N (Newton) is equal to 1 kg·m/s2. Therefore, the SI unit of linear momentum is correctly represented by N-s. The other options represent units for different physical quantities: N m is for torque, N-m2 is not a standard unit, and N/m is for stiffness or spring constant.

questions.text: A force which always opposes the motion of a body is called
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Static force refers to the force that keeps an object at rest. It is not specifically a force that opposes motion, but rather prevents the start of motion.
Gravitational force is an attractive force between two masses and does not specifically oppose motion; it acts to pull objects toward one another.
Frictional force is the correct answer as it is the force that always opposes the relative motion between two surfaces in contact.
Impulsive force is a force that acts for a short duration and changes the momentum of an object, not specifically opposing motion.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is the frictional force, which is a force that opposes the relative motion or tendency of such motion of two surfaces in contact. Friction acts in the opposite direction of movement, effectively slowing down or stopping moving objects. Static force refers to the force that counteracts initial motion, but it does not apply once an object is already moving. Gravitational force is a force that attracts two bodies toward each other, such as the pull between the Earth and objects on it, and does not specifically oppose motion. Impulsive force refers to a force that acts on an object for a very brief period, resulting in a change in momentum, rather than opposing motion.

questions.text: Stroke's law is applicable to
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Stoke's law is not applicable to motion on surfaces, as it deals with fluid dynamics rather than surface motion.
Motion through space generally involves minimal resistance, unlike the viscous resistance considered by Stoke's law.
This is correct as Stoke's law describes the drag force experienced by objects moving through a viscous fluid.
Stoke's law is not applicable in non-resistive media as it specifically addresses the resistance encountered in viscous fluids.

questions.explanation: Stoke's law applies to the motion of spherical objects moving through a viscous medium, where a drag force is proportional to the velocity and the viscosity of the medium. This law is not applicable to motion across horizontal surfaces, or in space, as these scenarios do not involve viscous drag. Similarly, it does not apply to non-resistive media, as the presence of resistance is a core component of the law.

questions.text: The acceleration of a spherical ball on a smooth inclined plane is maximum when the angle of inclination to the horizontal is
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: At 90 degrees, the inclined plane is vertical, and the ball would be in free fall with maximum acceleration due to gravity.
At 60 degrees, the ball is accelerating down the incline, but not as fast as it would in free fall.
At 30 degrees, the component of gravitational force along the plane is smaller compared to larger angles.
At 0 degrees, the plane is horizontal, and the ball does not accelerate along the plane.

questions.explanation: The acceleration of the spherical ball on a smooth inclined plane is determined by the component of gravitational force acting along the plane. This component is maximized when the plane is vertical, which occurs at an angle of 90 degrees. At this angle, the ball is essentially in free fall, experiencing maximum acceleration equal to the acceleration due to gravity (9.8 m/s²). At angles less than 90 degrees, the acceleration component along the plane is reduced because the gravitational force is divided between the component along the plane and the component perpendicular to it. At 0 degrees, the plane is horizontal, and there is no component of gravitational force acting to accelerate the ball along the plane, so the acceleration is zero.

questions.text: When a force of 4 N acts on a mass of 2 kg for a time 2 s, what is the rate of change of momentum?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This is incorrect. The rate of change of momentum is equal to the force applied, which is not 1 N in this case.
This is incorrect. The rate of change of momentum equals the force applied, which is not 2 N here.
Correct! The rate of change of momentum is equal to the force applied, which is 4 N.
This is incorrect. The rate of change of momentum should equal the applied force, which is not 8 N.

questions.explanation: The rate of change of momentum is equivalent to the force applied on an object, according to Newton's second law. In this scenario, a force of 4 N is applied to a mass of 2 kg. Therefore, the rate of change of momentum is 4 kg m s-2. The other options are incorrect as they do not match the force applied to the object.

questions.text: A force of 20 N acts on a body of mass 100 g. The acceleration produced in the body is
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This assumes incorrect conversion of mass, leading to an inaccurate calculation of acceleration.
This value is too low and might result from a misunderstanding of unit conversion.
This result is too high, potentially from incorrect unit conversion or calculation.
This is the correct acceleration after converting mass to kilograms and using Newton's second law.

questions.explanation: To find the acceleration, use Newton's second law: F = ma, where F is the force, m is the mass, and a is the acceleration. First, convert the mass from grams to kilograms: 100 g = 0.1 kg. Then, use the formula: a = F/m = 20 N / 0.1 kg = 200 m s-2. Thus, the correct answer is 200 m s-2. The other options result from incorrect unit conversions or calculations.

questions.text: if a body is at rest it may have
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Momentum is a product of mass and velocity. Since the body is at rest, its velocity is zero, and thus its momentum is also zero.
Velocity refers to the speed of something in a given direction. A body at rest has no movement, hence no velocity.
Acceleration is the rate of change of velocity. A stationary body does not experience a change in velocity, so its acceleration is zero.
Even if a body is at rest, it can still possess energy. For example, a body at a height has potential energy due to gravity.

questions.explanation: While a body at rest does not have momentum, velocity, or acceleration, it can still have energy in various forms. The most common is potential energy, which depends on factors like position or configuration. For instance, a stationary rock at the top of a hill possesses gravitational potential energy. On the other hand, momentum and velocity are directly linked to motion, and acceleration refers to changes in velocity, none of which apply to a body at rest.

questions.text: The rate of decrease in velocity is called
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Acceleration refers to the rate of change of velocity, but it specifically denotes an increase in velocity.
Momentum is the product of an object's mass and its velocity, not related to the rate of change of velocity.
Retardation, also known as negative acceleration, refers to the rate of decrease in velocity.
Centripetal acceleration is the rate of change of velocity directed towards the center of a circular path, not specifically a decrease in velocity.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Retardation. Retardation, or negative acceleration, describes the rate of decrease in velocity. While acceleration is the general term for changing velocity, it typically implies an increase. Momentum is unrelated to changes in velocity, as it involves mass and velocity together. Centripetal acceleration concerns objects moving in a circular path, focusing on changes in direction rather than a decrease in speed.

questions.text: A body is thrown upward with initial velocity 9.8 m/sec. It will go
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This is the correct height calculated using the formula for maximum height.
This assumes the body travels a distance equal to its initial velocity, which doesn't account for gravitational deceleration.
This height is twice the correct height, possibly confusing the initial velocity with height.
This option is three times the correct height, not factoring in gravitational deceleration.

questions.explanation: The maximum height a body reaches when thrown upwards can be calculated using the formula: h = u^2 / (2g), where u is the initial velocity and g is the acceleration due to gravity (approximately 9.8 m/s²). Substituting the given initial velocity of 9.8 m/s, we get: h = (9.8 m/s)^2 / (2 * 9.8 m/s²) = 4.9 m. Therefore, the body will reach a maximum height of 4.9 meters. Options B, C, and D do not correctly apply this formula, resulting in incorrect heights.

questions.text: It is easier to draw up a block along an inclined plane then to pick it up vertically because
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: While friction might be less of a factor in some cases, it is not the primary reason why it is easier to move a block up an inclined plane than lifting it vertically.
The mass of the block remains constant regardless of the path taken. It is not correct to say that the mass becomes smaller.

options.text: A part of the weight is overcome by the inclined plane
options.explanation: This is correct because the inclined plane allows the force required to lift the block to be spread over a longer distance, effectively reducing the force needed at any point.
The acceleration due to gravity (G) remains constant and does not change based on the path taken to move the block.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is that 'A part of the weight is overcome by the inclined plane.' This is due to the inclined plane allowing the force of gravity to be distributed across its length, requiring less force to move the block upward compared to lifting it vertically. The force needed to lift an object vertically equals its weight, but when using an inclined plane, the effective force is reduced because it only needs to counteract a component of the gravitational force.Option A is incorrect because friction might not always be reduced depending on the surface of the plane. Option B is incorrect because mass is an intrinsic property and does not change based on conditions. Option D is incorrect because the value of gravitational acceleration (G) does not change.

questions.text: Passengers should not stand in a moving bus because
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: The bus may not move smoothly
options.explanation: The smoothness of the bus's movement is not the primary concern for standing passenger safety during sudden stops.
While standing passengers can slightly affect stability, it is not the main reason for the safety warning against standing.

options.text: Passengers may be thrown forward during sudden stops
options.explanation: Inertia causes passengers to continue their motion even when the bus stops suddenly, making them prone to falling forward.

options.text: Standing passengers obstruct the view of others
options.explanation: Obstructing the view is not a safety issue and is not the reason for advising against standing.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is that passengers may be thrown forward during sudden stops. This is due to inertia, which states that an object in motion stays in motion unless acted upon by an external force. When the bus stops suddenly, standing passengers, who are not secured, will continue moving forward, increasing the risk of falling. Option A is incorrect because the smoothness of the bus's movement is not the concern here. Option B is incorrect as the stability of the bus isn't significantly compromised by standing passengers. Option D addresses a view obstruction issue, which does not relate to safety concerns.

questions.text: A sheet of paper can be withdrawn from under bottle of milk without dropping it if the paper is jerked out quickly. This is an example of:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Impulse is the change in momentum resulting from a force applied over time. It is not relevant here as the bottle's motion doesn't change significantly.
Weight is the gravitational force acting on an object. It does not explain why the bottle resists moving when the paper is pulled.
Acceleration refers to changes in velocity due to applied forces. The bottle does not undergo acceleration in this scenario.
Inertia is the resistance of an object to changes in its state of motion. The bottle remains at rest due to its inertia when the paper is quickly removed.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Inertia. Inertia is the property of matter that causes it to resist changes to its motion. In this scenario, the bottle remains stationary because its inertia resists the sudden change in motion when the paper is jerked out. This quick action does not provide enough time for the force to significantly alter the bottle's state of rest. The other options are incorrect: 'Impulse' involves a change in momentum over time, which does not occur here; 'Weight' pertains to gravitational forces, not motion resistance; and 'Acceleration' involves changes in velocity, which are not relevant to the bottle in this context.

questions.text: If the velocity of the body changes it may be termed as:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Acceleration is the rate at which the velocity of a body changes with time. It can be positive (increasing speed) or negative (deceleration).
Instantaneous velocity refers to the velocity of an object at a specific point in time, not the change in velocity.
Speed is the scalar magnitude of velocity and does not account for changes in direction or velocity.
Displacement is the vector quantity that denotes change in position, not change in velocity.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Acceleration. When the velocity of a body changes, it is experiencing acceleration, which is the rate of change of velocity over time. This change can be an increase in velocity (positive acceleration) or a decrease (negative acceleration, also called deceleration). Instantaneous velocity is simply a snapshot of velocity at a particular moment, not a change. Speed is a scalar and does not indicate a change in velocity. Magnitude of displacement refers to the change in position, not velocity.

questions.text: Inertia of a body is measured in terms of:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Weight is the force exerted by gravity on an object, not a measure of its inertia.
Applied force is an external influence on an object and does not measure its inertia.
Reaction refers to the response to an action, not a measure of inertia.
Mass is the measure of the amount of matter in an object, which directly relates to its inertia.

questions.explanation: Inertia is the property of an object to resist changes in its state of motion. It is directly proportional to the mass of the object. The greater the mass, the greater the inertia, meaning more force is required to change the object's motion. Thus, inertia is measured in terms of mass. Other options like weight, applied force, and reaction do not measure inertia. Weight depends on the gravitational pull on the mass, applied force is an external factor, and reaction is a response to an action, none of which quantify an object's inertia.

questions.text: Two bodies are moving in opposite directions with speed v. What is the magnitude of their relative velocity:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This is correct because when two bodies move in opposite directions, their relative velocity is the sum of their individual speeds.
This is incorrect because it does not account for the fact that the bodies are moving in opposite directions. The relative velocity should be greater than the speed of one object alone.
This is incorrect because dividing the speed by 2 suggests a reduction in speed, which does not apply to relative velocity in opposite directions.
This is incorrect because zero relative velocity implies that both bodies are stationary or moving at the same speed in the same direction. Here, they are moving in opposite directions.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is 2v. When two bodies are moving in opposite directions, their relative velocity is the sum of their speeds. So if each body is moving with a speed v, the relative velocity is v + v = 2v. The other options are incorrect because they either treat the speed as unchanged (option B), incorrectly halve it (option C), or suggest no motion (option D).

questions.text: A car starts from rest and travels forward with constant acceleration. Which of the following statements is true?
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: The power delivered by the engine decreases
options.explanation: This is incorrect because power is related to force and velocity. As the car accelerates, its velocity increases, leading to an increase in power.

options.text: The momentum of the car remains constant
options.explanation: This is incorrect because momentum is the product of mass and velocity. With constant acceleration, velocity increases, thus changing the momentum.

options.text: The power delivered by the engine increases
options.explanation: This is correct. With constant acceleration, the car's velocity increases over time, which means the power (force multiplied by velocity) also increases.

options.text: The power delivered by the engine remains constant
options.explanation: This is incorrect. If the car is accelerating, its velocity is increasing, leading to an increase in power delivered.

questions.explanation: As a car accelerates from rest with constant acceleration, its velocity increases over time. Power is calculated as the product of force and velocity. Since the velocity is increasing, the power delivered by the engine must also increase to maintain that acceleration. Option C is correct because it reflects this relationship between acceleration and increasing power. Option A is incorrect because the power does not decrease; it increases due to the rise in velocity. Option B is incorrect because the momentum is not constant; it changes with the increase in velocity. Option D is incorrect because the power does not remain constant; it increases as the car accelerates.

questions.text: A train is brought to a sudden stop its:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Acceleration is not reduced to zero when the train stops suddenly; it is actually negative as it decelerates to a stop.
When a train comes to a complete stop, its velocity is reduced to zero.

options.text: Average acceleration is reduced to zero
options.explanation: Average acceleration is not reduced to zero when stopping suddenly; it indicates a change in velocity over time, which is not zero.
The statement is incorrect because 'Velocity is reduced to zero' is a correct option.

questions.explanation: When a train is brought to a sudden stop, its velocity is reduced to zero. Velocity refers to the speed of an object in a given direction. As the train stops, it no longer moves, making its velocity zero. Option A is incorrect because acceleration is not zero; it is negative as the train decelerates. Option C is incorrect because average acceleration, representing the rate of change of velocity, is not zero when stopping suddenly. Option D is incorrect because there is a correct statement among the options, which is Option B.

questions.text: A body moving with constant velocity be:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: A body changing its direction is not moving with constant velocity, as velocity is a vector quantity that includes both speed and direction.
A body moving with constant velocity is in a state of equilibrium as there is no net force acting on it.
Acceleration refers to a change in velocity. A body moving with constant velocity is not accelerating.
A body traveling in a circle undergoes centripetal acceleration due to continuous change in direction, thus not moving with constant velocity.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is B: in equilibrium. A body moving with constant velocity is in a state of dynamic equilibrium, meaning there is no net force acting on it, according to Newton's First Law of Motion. Option A is incorrect because a change in direction implies a change in velocity. Option C is incorrect because acceleration means a change in velocity, either in magnitude or direction. Option D is incorrect because moving in a circle involves centripetal acceleration, which means the velocity is not constant.

questions.text: A body is accelerated from rest to a certain distance, and then decelerates up to a certain distance, which of the following graph shows the above case:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This graph shows a constant velocity, which does not represent any acceleration or deceleration.
This graph only shows deceleration without any initial acceleration from rest.
This graph correctly illustrates an initial acceleration from rest followed by a deceleration.
This graph shows an acceleration followed by a constant velocity, with no deceleration.

questions.explanation: The correct graph is Option C. It accurately represents the motion of an object that accelerates from rest, travels a certain distance, and then decelerates. The graph should show a slope increasing from zero (indicating acceleration from rest), then decreasing (indicating deceleration). Option A shows constant velocity, Option B shows only deceleration, and Option D shows acceleration without subsequent deceleration.

questions.text: Two freely falling objects, one 10 kg and 20 g are dropped from the same height at the same time. Air resistance is negligible. Which of the following statements is (are) true:i) Both objects have the same potential energy at the top.ii) Both objects fall with the same acceleration.iii) Both objects have the same speed just before hitting the ground.
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Potential energy depends on mass, so this statement is incorrect.
This statement is true, but it is not the only correct statement.
Both objects have the same speed just before hitting the ground, but there's more to consider.
Correct! Both objects fall with the same acceleration and have the same speed just before hitting the ground.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is II and III only. Both objects experience the same gravitational acceleration of approximately 9.81 m/s², regardless of their mass. This uniform acceleration causes them to reach the same speed just before impact. However, potential energy is calculated using PE = mgh, where m is mass, g is gravitational acceleration, and h is height. Since the masses differ, the potential energies at the top are different. Therefore, statement I is incorrect. Statements II and III are correct as the acceleration due to gravity is consistent, and objects in free fall reach the same speed before hitting the ground when air resistance is negligible.

questions.text: Terminal velocity is usually defined as the:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Shock waves are related to sudden changes in pressure and velocity, not terminal velocity.
This refers to the speed at which light travels through water, not related to terminal velocity.

options.text: Velocity at which air resistance balances gravity
options.explanation: This is the correct definition of terminal velocity. It occurs when the force of gravity is balanced by the force of air resistance, resulting in constant velocity.
Not all options can be correct as terminal velocity specifically refers to the balance between air resistance and gravity.

questions.explanation: Terminal velocity is the constant speed that a freely falling object eventually reaches when the resistance of the medium through which it is falling prevents further acceleration. At this point, the downward force of gravity (weight) is exactly balanced by the upward force of drag (air resistance), making the net force on the object zero and resulting in no further acceleration. Option A is incorrect because shock waves concern the speed of sound and pressure changes, not terminal velocity. Option B is incorrect as it describes the speed of light in a medium, unrelated to falling objects. Option D is incorrect because not all the options can simultaneously define terminal velocity correctly.

questions.text: The velocity of falling rain drop attains limited value because of:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Air resistance acts in the opposite direction to the motion of the raindrop, slowing it down until it reaches a constant velocity.
Upthrust is a buoyant force that acts in a fluid, but it is not significant in the context of a falling raindrop.
While air can exert a viscous force, the primary force that limits the speed of the raindrop is air resistance rather than viscosity.
Not all these forces play a role in limiting the velocity of the raindrop. The primary force is air resistance.

questions.explanation: The velocity of a falling raindrop reaches a limited, constant value called terminal velocity due to air resistance. As the raindrop falls, it accelerates until the force of air resistance equals the gravitational force acting on it. At this point, the net force on the raindrop is zero, and it continues to fall at a constant velocity. Other forces like upthrust and viscous force do not significantly impact the raindrop's speed in this context. Therefore, the correct option is air resistance.

questions.text: A penny and a piece of paper are simultaneously released from the top of a vertical evacuated time, which one of the following statements is correct:
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: The penny and paper remain at rest.
options.explanation: Objects remain at rest unless a force acts on them. In this scenario, both objects are released, not at rest.

options.text: The penny and paper strike the base of the tube at the same time.
options.explanation: In a vacuum, there is no air resistance, so both objects fall at the same rate due to gravity alone.

options.text: The penny strikes the base of the tube before the paper.
options.explanation: This would only be true if air resistance affected the fall, but in a vacuum, there is no air resistance.

options.text: The penny strikes the base of the tube after the paper.
options.explanation: In a vacuum, gravity affects all objects equally regardless of their mass or shape.

questions.explanation: In a vacuum, there is no air resistance, allowing all objects to fall at the same rate due to gravity alone. This means that both the penny and the paper will strike the base of the tube at the same time. Option B is correct because it accounts for the lack of air resistance in an evacuated environment. Option A is incorrect because the objects are released, not at rest. Options C and D are incorrect because they suggest a difference in the time it takes for the objects to fall, which would only occur if air resistance were a factor.

questions.text: A body falling freely under the action of gravity has:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This suggests that the weight decreases when falling, which is not accurate for free fall.
The weight doesn't increase during free fall; it remains constant because it's the force of gravity acting on the body.
In free fall, a body appears to have no weight because it is in a state of weightlessness, experiencing only the force of gravity.
The weight of the body does not change during free fall; it is the sensation of weight that changes.

questions.explanation: In free fall, a body experiences weightlessness because it is accelerating due to gravity alone, with no support forces acting on it. This creates the sensation that the body has 'no weight,' even though gravity is acting on it. Option C is correct as it reflects this state of weightlessness. Options A and B are incorrect because the weight of the body remains constant, determined by the gravitational force. Option D is incorrect because the weight does not vary; instead, the perception of weight changes due to the absence of contact forces.

questions.text: A boy sitting in train moving at a constant velocity throws a ball straight up into the air the ball will drop:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The ball will land back in his hand because both the boy and the ball share the train's velocity.
This would only happen if the train or the boy accelerated, which is not the case here.
This would occur if the train suddenly decelerated, which does not happen in this scenario.
The ball cannot land beside him as there is no lateral force acting on it.

questions.explanation: When the ball is thrown up inside a moving train, it maintains the horizontal velocity of the train. Because the train moves at a constant velocity and no horizontal forces act on the ball, it will land back in the boy's hand. The train's motion doesn't affect the vertical motion of the ball. Other options such as landing in front, behind, or beside the boy would require additional forces or changes in velocity, which are not present in this scenario.

questions.text: If the graph between mass and acceleration is a straight line then:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: According to Newton's Second Law, when force is constant, acceleration is inversely proportional to mass. Thus, a graph of mass versus acceleration being a straight line indicates this inverse relationship.
This implies a direct proportionality between acceleration and mass, which contradicts the inverse relationship described by Newton's Second Law when force is constant.
This suggests a squared inverse relationship between mass and acceleration, which is not consistent with Newton's Second Law for a constant force scenario.
This indicates a squared relationship involving mass, which does not align with the linear inverse relationship expected from Newton's Second Law when force is constant.

questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law of Motion states that force (F) equals mass (m) times acceleration (a), or F = ma. When the force is constant, this relationship can be rearranged to show that acceleration (a) is inversely proportional to mass (m), i.e., a α (1/m). Therefore, a graph depicting a straight line between mass and acceleration indicates this inverse relationship, making Option A correct. Option B is incorrect because it suggests a direct proportionality that does not hold true under these conditions. Options C and D introduce non-linear or squared relationships that are not applicable in this context of constant force.

questions.text: Swimming is possible on account of:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This law explains how an object remains at rest or in uniform motion unless acted upon by an external force. It doesn't directly explain swimming.
This law relates force, mass, and acceleration. While relevant to motion, it doesn't directly explain the propulsion mechanism in swimming.
This law states that for every action, there is an equal and opposite reaction. It explains how swimmers push against the water to move forward.
This law describes the gravitational attraction between masses, which is not primarily responsible for swimming.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is the Third law of motion. This law, known as the action-reaction law, explains how swimmers use their arms and legs to push against the water, and in turn, the water pushes back, propelling them forward. The First law of motion deals with inertia, which is not directly related to the act of swimming. The Second law of motion relates to how force causes acceleration, which is important in understanding motion but does not specifically describe the mechanism of swimming. Finally, Newton's law of gravitation describes gravitational forces and is not responsible for the propulsion in swimming.

questions.text: The variation of speed with time is shown in the following graph. The body has greatest acceleration between points:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The slope between A and B is moderate, indicating a moderate acceleration.
The slope between B and C is the steepest, indicating the greatest acceleration.
The slope between C and D is less steep compared to B and C, indicating a smaller acceleration.
The slope between D and E is the least steep or possibly zero, indicating little to no acceleration.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is B and C, because acceleration is determined by the steepness of the graph's slope. The steeper the slope, the greater the acceleration. Between B and C, the slope is the steepest, indicating the highest acceleration. Other sections of the graph have less steep slopes or are horizontal, indicating lower acceleration or constant speed.

questions.text: The average and instantaneous velocities will be equal when a body moves with:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: With uniform velocity, both speed and direction remain constant, ensuring that the average velocity over any time interval matches the instantaneous velocity at any point.
Constant acceleration implies a continuous change in velocity, which results in differences between the average and instantaneous velocities.
Variable velocity indicates fluctuations in speed or direction, causing the average and instantaneous velocities to differ at various points in time.
Retardation refers to negative acceleration, where the velocity is decreasing, leading to a variation between average and instantaneous velocities.

questions.explanation: The average and instantaneous velocities are equal when a body moves with uniform velocity. This means the body maintains a constant speed and direction, resulting in no variation over time. Therefore, at any given point, the instantaneous velocity matches the average velocity calculated over any interval. In contrast, with constant acceleration, the velocity is continuously changing, leading to differences between average and instantaneous velocities. Similarly, variable velocity implies fluctuations in speed or direction, and retardation involves a decrease in speed, both of which cause the average and instantaneous velocities to differ.

questions.text: If the velocity time graph of a moving body is a curve, the body moves with:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: A constant speed would result in a horizontal line on a speed-time graph, not a curve.
Constant velocity implies no change in speed or direction, which would be shown as a straight line on a velocity-time graph.
Constant acceleration would be represented by a straight line with a constant slope on a velocity-time graph.
A curve on a velocity-time graph indicates that the slope is changing, which means the acceleration is not constant.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is changing acceleration. A velocity-time graph that is a curve indicates that the slope is continuously changing. This change in slope signifies that the acceleration is not constant but varying over time. In contrast, a straight line on a velocity-time graph would indicate constant acceleration. Constant speed and constant velocity would appear as horizontal lines, either on a speed-time or velocity-time graph respectively, which are not curves.
